qtype: Ratio division, currency exchange, cost calculation, fractions of a quantity
tex:
Adele, Barbara and Collette share \$680 in the ratio 9 : 7 : 4.
\newline

(a) Show that Adele receives \$306.
\newline

(b) Calculate the amount that Barbara and Collette each receives.
\newline

Barbara \$ 

Collette \$ 

(c) Adele changes her \$306 into euros (€) when the exchange rate is €1 = \$1.125 .
\newline

Calculate the number of euros she receives.
\newline

€ 

(d) Barbara spends a total of \$17.56 on 5 kg of apples and 3 kg of bananas.
\newline
Apples cost \$2.69 per kilogram.
\newline

Calculate the cost per kilogram of bananas.
\newline

\$ 

(e) Collette spends half of her share on clothes and $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of her share on books.
\newline

Calculate the amount she has left.
\newline

\$

content.content:
Show that Adele receives \$306.
\newline

Calculate the amount that Barbara and Collette each receives.
\newline

Barbara \$ \AnswerLine[][]
\AnswerLine[][]
Collette \$ \AnswerLine[][]

Adele changes her \$306 into euros (€) when the exchange rate is €1 = \$1.125 .
\newline

Calculate the number of euros she receives.
\newline

€ \AnswerLine[][]

Barbara spends a total of \$17.56 on 5 kg of apples and 3 kg of bananas.
\newline
Apples cost \$2.69 per kilogram.
\newline

Calculate the cost per kilogram of bananas.
\newline

\$ \AnswerLine[][]

Collette spends half of her share on clothes and $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of her share on books.
\newline

Calculate the amount she has left.
\newline

\$ \AnswerLine[][]

Adele, Barbara and Collette share \$680 in the ratio 9 : 7 : 4.
\newline

_rawBody:
Adele, Barbara and Collette share \$680 in the ratio 9 : 7 : 4.

\begin{parts}
\item Show that Adele receives \$306.

\AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{1}

\item Calculate the amount that Barbara and Collette each receives.

Barbara \$ \AnswerLine[][]
\AnswerLine[][]
Collette \$ \AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{3}

\item Adele changes her \$306 into euros (€) when the exchange rate is €1 = \$1.125 .

Calculate the number of euros she receives.

€ \AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{2}

\item Barbara spends a total of \$17.56 on 5 kg of apples and 3 kg of bananas.
Apples cost \$2.69 per kilogram.

Calculate the cost per kilogram of bananas.

\$ \AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{3}

\item Collette spends half of her share on clothes and $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of her share on books.

Calculate the amount she has left.

\$ \AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{3}

\end{parts}

tex:
The scale drawing shows two boundaries, AB and BC, of a field ABCD.
\newline
The scale of the drawing is 1 cm represents 8 m.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

(a) The boundaries CD and AD of the field are each 72 m long.
\newline

(i) (b) Work out the length of CD and AD on the scale drawing.
\newline

(c) Using a ruler and compasses only, complete accurately the scale drawing of the field.
\newline

(d) A tree in the field is

• equidistant from A and B
and
• equidistant from AB and BC.
\newline

On the scale drawing, construct two lines to find the position of the tree.
\newline
Use a straight edge and compasses only and leave in your construction arcs.
\newline

tex:
(a) The price of a house decreased from \$82 500 to \$77 500.
\newline

Calculate the percentage decrease.
\newline

(b) Roland invests \$12 000 in an account that pays compound interest at a rate of 2.2\% per year.
\newline

Calculate the value of his investment at the end of 6 years.
\newline
Give your answer correct to the nearest dollar.
\newline

\$

content.content:
The price of a house decreased from \$82 500 to \$77 500.
\newline

Calculate the percentage decrease.
\newline

Roland invests \$12 000 in an account that pays compound interest at a rate of 2.2\% per year.
\newline

Calculate the value of his investment at the end of 6 years.
\newline
Give your answer correct to the nearest dollar.
\newline

\$ \AnswerLine[][]

qtype: factorisation by grouping, difference of squares, quadratic factorisation

tex:
A solid hemisphere has volume 230 cm$^{3}$.
\newline

(a) Calculate the radius of the hemisphere.
\newline
[The volume, V, of a sphere with radius r is V = $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$.
\newline
]

(b) A solid cylinder with radius 1.6 cm is attached to the hemisphere to make a toy.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

The total volume of the toy is 300 cm$^{3}$.
\newline

(i) (c) Calculate the height of the cylinder.
\newline

(d) A mathematically similar toy has volume 19 200 cm$^{3}$.
\newline

Calculate the radius of the cylinder for this toy.
\newline

content.content:
Calculate the radius of the hemisphere.
\newline
[The volume, V, of a sphere with radius r is V = $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$.
\newline
]

Calculate the height of the cylinder.
\newline

A mathematically similar toy has volume 19 200 cm$^{3}$.
\newline

Calculate the radius of the cylinder for this toy.
\newline

stem.content:
A solid hemisphere has volume 230 cm$^{3}$.
\newline

\newline

\newline

(g) By drawing a suitable straight line, solve the equation $x^{3} + 5x + 2 = 0$ for $-1.5 \leq x \leq 1.5$.
\newline

tex:
The probability that it will rain tomorrow is $\dfrac{5}{8}$.
\newline

If it rains, the probability that Rafael walks to school is $\dfrac{1}{6}$.
\newline

If it does not rain, the probability that Rafael walks to school is $\dfrac{7}{10}$.
\newline

(a) Complete the tree diagram.
\newline

(b) Calculate the probability that it will rain tomorrow and Rafael walks to school.
\newline

(c) Calculate the probability that Rafael does not walk to school.
\newline

content.content:
Complete the tree diagram.
\newline

Calculate the probability that it will rain tomorrow and Rafael walks to school.
\newline

Calculate the probability that Rafael does not walk to school.
\newline

stem.content:
The probability that it will rain tomorrow is $\dfrac{5}{8}$.
\newline

If it rains, the probability that Rafael walks to school is $\dfrac{1}{6}$.
\newline

If it does not rain, the probability that Rafael walks to school is $\dfrac{7}{10}$.
\newline

qtype: Reverse percentage, upper/lower bounds calculation, Venn diagram
tex:
(a) In 2017, the membership fee for a sports club was \$79.50.
\newline
This was an increase of 6\% on the fee in 2016.
\newline

Calculate the fee in 2016.
\newline

\$ 

(b) On one day, the number of members using the exercise machines was 40, correct to the nearest 10.
\newline
Each member used a machine for 30 minutes, correct to the nearest 5 minutes.
\newline

Calculate the lower bound for the number of minutes the exercise machines were used on this day.
\newline

(c) On another day, the number of members using the exercise machines (E), the swimming pool (S) and the tennis courts (T) is shown on the Venn diagram.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

(i) (d) Find the number of members using only the tennis courts.
\newline

(e) Find the number of members using the swimming pool.
\newline

(f) A member using the swimming pool is chosen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that this member also uses the tennis courts and the exercise machines.
\newline

(g) Find $n(T \cap E)$.
\newline

content.content:
In 2017, the membership fee for a sports club was \$79.50.
\newline
This was an increase of 6\% on the fee in 2016.
\newline

Calculate the fee in 2016.
\newline

\$ \AnswerLine[][]

On one day, the number of members using the exercise machines was 40, correct to the nearest 10.
\newline
Each member used a machine for 30 minutes, correct to the nearest 5 minutes.
\newline

Calculate the lower bound for the number of minutes the exercise machines were used on this day.
\newline

Find the number of members using only the tennis courts.
\newline

Find the number of members using the swimming pool.
\newline

A member using the swimming pool is chosen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that this member also uses the tennis courts and the exercise machines.
\newline

Find $n(T \cap E)$.
\newline

tex:
Marco is making patterns with grey and white circular mats.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

The patterns form a sequence.
\newline
Marco makes a table to show some information about the patterns.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{c|c|c}
Pattern number & Number of grey mats & Total number of mats \\
\hline
1 & 6 & 6 \\
2 & 9 & 10 \\
3 & 12 & 15 \\
4 & 15 & 21 \\
5 & & \\
\end{tabular}

(a) Complete the table for Pattern 5.
\newline

(b) Find an expression, in terms of n, for the number of grey mats in Pattern n.
\newline

(c) Marco makes a pattern with 24 grey mats.
\newline
Find the total number of mats in this pattern.
\newline

(d) Marco needs a total of 6 mats to make the first pattern.
\newline
He needs a total of 16 mats to make the first two patterns.
\newline
He needs a total of $\dfrac{1}{6}n^{3} + an^{2} + bn$ mats to make the first n patterns.
\newline
Find the value of a and the value of b.
\newline

a = 
b =
